content:
[S1]: Antibiotic resistance is spreading because bacteria are evolving to survive the medicines designed to kill them.  
[READER]: Curious but a bit overwhelmed; wants a simple explanation of why this evolution happens. Needs a clear cause.  

[S2]: This happens when antibiotics are used too often or incorrectly, giving bacteria more chances to adapt.  
[READER]: Understands overuse is a problem, wants examples or situations to relate to.  

[S3]: For example, when patients don’t finish their full course of antibiotics, some bacteria survive and become stronger.  
[READER]: Nods along, sees the practical impact; wants to know how widespread or serious this issue is.  

[S4]: As a result, infections become harder and more expensive to treat, putting pressure on healthcare systems and businesses.  
[READER]: Concerned about costs and impact on operations; wants to know who is responsible and what can be done.  

[S5]: Overprescribing antibiotics by doctors and their use in livestock farming are major contributors to this problem.  
[READER]: Interested in accountability; wants to know what actions are effective or already underway.  

[S6]: Efforts to control antibiotic resistance include better prescription practices and reducing unnecessary use in agriculture.  
[READER]: Appreciates practical steps; wants to understand the urgency and risks if nothing changes.  

[S7]: If resistance continues to spread, common infections could once again become deadly, threatening public health and economies.  
[READER]: Alarmed and motivated; wants to know how they or their company can help or stay informed.  

[S8]: Staying informed, supporting responsible antibiotic use, and promoting innovation in medicine are key ways to combat this growing threat.  
[READER]: Reassured by a call to action; ready to consider their role and influence in solutions.  

===BEGIN===  
Antibiotic resistance is spreading because bacteria are evolving to survive the medicines designed to kill them. This happens when antibiotics are used too often or incorrectly, giving bacteria more chances to adapt. For example, when patients don’t finish their full course of antibiotics, some bacteria survive and become stronger. As a result, infections become harder and more expensive to treat, putting pressure on healthcare systems and businesses. Overprescribing antibiotics by doctors and their use in livestock farming are major contributors to this problem. Efforts to control antibiotic resistance include better prescription practices and reducing unnecessary use in agriculture. If resistance continues to spread, common infections could once again become deadly, threatening public health and economies. Staying informed, supporting responsible antibiotic use, and promoting innovation in medicine are key ways to combat this growing threat.  
===END===
